an9.36:1.1: "I say, *bhikkhus*, that the destruction of the *āsavas* depends on the first *jhāna*;
an9.36:1.2: I say, *bhikkhus*, that the destruction of the *āsavas* depends on the second *jhāna*;
an9.36:1.3: I say, *bhikkhus*, that the destruction of the *āsavas* depends on the third *jhāna*;
an9.36:1.4: I say, *bhikkhus*, that the destruction of the *āsavas* depends on the fourth *jhāna*;
an9.36:1.5: I say, *bhikkhus*, that the destruction of the *āsavas* depends on the dimension of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatana*];
an9.36:1.6: I say, *bhikkhus*, that the destruction of the *āsavas* depends on the dimension of infinite consciousness [*viññāṇañcāyatana*];
an9.36:1.7: I say, *bhikkhus*, that the destruction of the *āsavas* depends on the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatana*];
an9.36:1.8: I say, *bhikkhus*, that the destruction of the *āsavas* depends on the dimension of neither-perception-nor-non-perception [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatana*];
an9.36:1.9: I say, *bhikkhus*, that the destruction of the *āsavas* depends on the cessation of perception and *vedanā* [*saññāvedayitanirodha*].
an9.36:10.1: Thus, *bhikkhus*, as far as the attainments of *saññā* extend, so far extends the breakthrough to final knowledge.
an9.36:10.2: Now as for these two dimensions that are to be relied upon —
an9.36:10.3: the attainment of the dimension of neither-perception-nor-non-perception [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatana*] and the cessation of perception and *vedanā* [*saññāvedayitanirodha*] — these, I say, *bhikkhus*, are to be rightly described by those who meditate, who are skilled in attainment, who are skilled in emerging from attainment, having entered and emerged from them."
an9.36:2.1: 'I say, *bhikkhus*, that the destruction of the *āsavas* depends on the first *jhāna*' — so it was said.
an9.36:2.2: In reference to what was this said?
an9.36:2.3: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu*, quite secluded from sensual pleasures …, enters and dwells in the first *jhāna*.
an9.36:2.4: Whatever is there of form [*rūpa*], *vedanā*, *saññā*, *saṅkhāras*, and *viññāṇa*, he contemplates those *dhammas* as impermanent [*aniccā*], as *dukkha*, as illness, as a boil, as a dart, as misery, as affliction, as alien, as disintegrating, as empty, as not-self [*anattā*].
an9.36:2.5: He turns his mind [*citta*] away from those *dhammas*.
an9.36:2.6: Having turned his mind [*citta*] away from those *dhammas*, he directs his mind [*citta*] toward the deathless element:
an9.36:2.7: 'This is peaceful, this is sublime — namely, the stilling of all *saṅkhāras*, the relinquishing of all acquisitions, the destruction of *taṇhā*, dispassion, cessation, *nibbāna*.'
an9.36:2.8: Established there, he attains the destruction of the *āsavas*.
an9.36:2.9: If he does not attain the destruction of the *āsavas*, then, by that very passion for *dhamma*, that delight in *dhamma*, with the destruction of the five lower fetters, he becomes one who arises spontaneously and there attains final *nibbāna*, not liable to return from that world.
an9.36:3.1: Suppose, *bhikkhus*, an archer or an archer's apprentice were to practice on a straw man or a mound of clay; in time he would become one who shoots far, who shoots swiftly, and who pierces large bodies.
an9.36:3.10: it was in reference to this that it was said.
an9.36:3.2: So too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu*, quite secluded from sensual pleasures …, enters and dwells in the first *jhāna*.
an9.36:3.3: Whatever is there of form [*rūpa*], *vedanā*, *saññā*, *saṅkhāras*, and *viññāṇa*, he contemplates those *dhammas* as impermanent [*aniccā*], as *dukkha*, as illness, as a boil, as a dart, as misery, as affliction, as alien, as disintegrating, as empty, as not-self [*anattā*].
an9.36:3.4: He turns his mind [*citta*] away from those *dhammas*.
an9.36:3.5: Having turned his mind [*citta*] away from those *dhammas*, he directs his mind [*citta*] toward the deathless element:
an9.36:3.6: 'This is peaceful, this is sublime — namely, the stilling of all *saṅkhāras*, the relinquishing of all acquisitions, the destruction of *taṇhā*, dispassion, cessation, *nibbāna*.'
an9.36:3.7: Established there, he attains the destruction of the *āsavas*.
an9.36:3.8: If he does not attain the destruction of the *āsavas*, then, by that very passion for *dhamma*, that delight in *dhamma*, with the destruction of the five lower fetters, he becomes one who arises spontaneously and there attains final *nibbāna*, not liable to return from that world.
an9.36:3.9: 'I say, *bhikkhus*, that the destruction of the *āsavas* depends on the first *jhāna*' —
an9.36:4.1: I say, *bhikkhus*, that the destruction of the *āsavas* depends on the second *jhāna* …
an9.36:4.10: Established there, he attains the destruction of the *āsavas*.
an9.36:4.11: If he does not attain the destruction of the *āsavas*, then, by that very passion for *dhamma*, that delight in *dhamma*, with the destruction of the five lower fetters, he becomes one who arises spontaneously and there attains final *nibbāna*, not liable to return from that world.
an9.36:4.3: 'I say, *bhikkhus*, that the destruction of the *āsavas* depends on the fourth *jhāna*' — so it was said.
an9.36:4.4: In reference to what was this said?
an9.36:4.5: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu*, with the abandoning of pleasure and pain, and with the earlier passing away of joy and grief, enters and dwells in the fourth *jhāna*, which has neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha*, and purity of *sati* and *upekkhā*.
an9.36:4.6: Whatever is there of form [*rūpa*], *vedanā*, *saññā*, *saṅkhāras*, and *viññāṇa*, he contemplates those *dhammas* as impermanent [*aniccā*], as *dukkha*, as illness, as a boil, as a dart, as misery, as affliction, as alien, as disintegrating, as empty, as not-self [*anattā*].
an9.36:4.7: He turns his mind [*citta*] away from those *dhammas*.
an9.36:4.8: Having turned his mind [*citta*] away from those *dhammas*, he directs his mind [*citta*] toward the deathless element:
an9.36:4.9: 'This is peaceful, this is sublime — namely, the stilling of all *saṅkhāras*, the relinquishing of all acquisitions, the destruction of *taṇhā*, dispassion, cessation, *nibbāna*.'
an9.36:5.1: Suppose, *bhikkhus*, an archer or an archer's apprentice were to practice on a straw man or a mound of clay; in time he would become one who shoots far, who shoots swiftly, and who pierces large bodies.
an9.36:5.2: So too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu*, with the abandoning of pleasure and pain …, enters and dwells in the fourth *jhāna*.
an9.36:5.4: … not liable to return from that world.
an9.36:5.5: 'I say, *bhikkhus*, that the destruction of the *āsavas* depends on the fourth *jhāna*' — it was in reference to this that it was said.
an9.36:6.1: 'I say, *bhikkhus*, that the destruction of the *āsavas* depends on the dimension of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatana*]' — so it was said.
an9.36:6.2: In reference to what was this said?
an9.36:6.3: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu*, by completely transcending *saññā* of form [*rūpa*], with the passing away of *saññā* of resistance, not attending to *saññā* of diversity, perceiving 'space is infinite', enters and dwells in the dimension of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatana*].
an9.36:6.4: Whatever is there of *vedanā*, *saññā*, *saṅkhāras*, and *viññāṇa*, he contemplates those *dhammas* as impermanent [*aniccā*], as *dukkha*, as illness, as a boil, as a dart, as misery, as affliction, as alien, as disintegrating, as empty, as not-self [*anattā*].
an9.36:6.5: He turns his mind [*citta*] away from those *dhammas*.
an9.36:6.6: Having turned his mind [*citta*] away from those *dhammas*, he directs his mind [*citta*] toward the deathless element:
an9.36:6.7: 'This is peaceful, this is sublime — namely, the stilling of all *saṅkhāras*, the relinquishing of all acquisitions, the destruction of *taṇhā*, dispassion, cessation, *nibbāna*.'
an9.36:6.8: Established there, he attains the destruction of the *āsavas*.
an9.36:6.9: If he does not attain the destruction of the *āsavas*, then, by that very passion for *dhamma*, that delight in *dhamma*, with the destruction of the five lower fetters, he becomes one who arises spontaneously and there attains final *nibbāna*, not liable to return from that world.
an9.36:7.1: Suppose, *bhikkhus*, an archer or an archer's apprentice were to practice on a straw man or a mound of clay; in time he would become one who shoots far, who shoots swiftly, and who pierces large bodies.
an9.36:7.2: So too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu*, by completely transcending *saññā* of form [*rūpa*], with the passing away of *saññā* of resistance, not attending to *saññā* of diversity, perceiving 'space is infinite', enters and dwells in the dimension of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatana*].
an9.36:7.4: … not liable to return from that world.
an9.36:7.5: 'I say, *bhikkhus*, that the destruction of the *āsavas* depends on the dimension of infinite space [*ākāsānañcāyatana*]' —
an9.36:7.6: it was in reference to this that it was said.
an9.36:8.1: 'I say, *bhikkhus*, that the destruction of the *āsavas* depends on the dimension of infinite consciousness [*viññāṇañcāyatana*]' …
an9.36:8.10: If he does not attain the destruction of the *āsavas*, then, by that very passion for *dhamma*, that delight in *dhamma*, with the destruction of the five lower fetters, he becomes one who arises spontaneously and there attains final *nibbāna*, not liable to return from that world.
an9.36:8.2: 'I say, *bhikkhus*, that the destruction of the *āsavas* depends on the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatana*]' — so it was said.
an9.36:8.3: In reference to what was this said?
an9.36:8.4: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu*, by completely transcending the dimension of infinite consciousness [*viññāṇañcāyatana*], perceiving 'there is nothing', enters and dwells in the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatana*].
an9.36:8.5: Whatever is there of *vedanā*, *saññā*, *saṅkhāras*, and *viññāṇa*, he contemplates those *dhammas* as impermanent [*aniccā*], as *dukkha*, as illness, as a boil, as a dart, as misery, as affliction, as alien, as disintegrating, as empty, as not-self [*anattā*].
an9.36:8.6: He turns his mind [*citta*] away from those *dhammas*.
an9.36:8.7: Having turned his mind [*citta*] away from those *dhammas*, he directs his mind [*citta*] toward the deathless element:
an9.36:8.8: 'This is peaceful, this is sublime — namely, the stilling of all *saṅkhāras*, the relinquishing of all acquisitions, the destruction of *taṇhā*, dispassion, cessation, *nibbāna*.'
an9.36:8.9: Established there, he attains the destruction of the *āsavas*.
an9.36:9.1: Suppose, *bhikkhus*, an archer or an archer's apprentice were to practice on a straw man or a mound of clay; in time he would become one who shoots far, who shoots swiftly, and who pierces large bodies.
an9.36:9.10: it was in reference to this that it was said.
an9.36:9.2: So too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu*, by completely transcending the dimension of infinite consciousness [*viññāṇañcāyatana*], perceiving 'there is nothing', enters and dwells in the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatana*].
an9.36:9.3: Whatever is there of *vedanā*, *saññā*, *saṅkhāras*, and *viññāṇa*, he contemplates those *dhammas* as impermanent [*aniccā*], as *dukkha*, as illness, as a boil, as a dart, as misery, as affliction, as alien, as disintegrating, as empty, as not-self [*anattā*].
an9.36:9.4: He turns his mind [*citta*] away from those *dhammas*.
an9.36:9.5: Having turned his mind [*citta*] away from those *dhammas*, he directs his mind [*citta*] toward the deathless element:
an9.36:9.6: 'This is peaceful, this is sublime — namely, the stilling of all *saṅkhāras*, the relinquishing of all acquisitions, the destruction of *taṇhā*, dispassion, cessation, *nibbāna*.'
an9.36:9.7: Established there, he attains the destruction of the *āsavas*.
an9.36:9.8: If he does not attain the destruction of the *āsavas*, then, by that very passion for *dhamma*, that delight in *dhamma*, with the destruction of the five lower fetters, he becomes one who arises spontaneously and there attains final *nibbāna*, not liable to return from that world.
an9.36:9.9: 'I say that the destruction of the *āsavas* depends on the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatana*]' —
